data.title: As Sentiment Softens, OneSpan's Cash Flow And Dividends Stand Out
data.description:
Market sentiment seems to be shifting dramatically in technology, particularly as it relates to Internet software and services companies. While the megacap leviathans are forever testing record highs, those following the 52-week lows are finding familiar names appearing. The market may have an endless appetite for the Alphabets (GOOGL) and Microsofts (MSFT) of the world, but smaller companies focused on single markets are decidedly out of favor right now.

From a contrarian perspective, this is a potential opportunity, because while there are a lot of dot-com companies that still struggle with an identity and a business model, there are companies here that actually are generating respectable free cash flow and doing a responsible job of returning that to shareholders. The company I want to take a look at today is OneSpan (OSPN).

Originally known as VASCO Data Security International, OneSpan is heavily in the security and authentication business. Their historic claim to fame was the DigiPass two-factor authentication devices, though in recent years we see that hardware-based solutions are falling in popularity and digital solutions are favored.

We’ll be looking at OneSpan’s efforts to redefine itself as less of a hardware company, how that is impacting their margins, and, importantly, how the company is using the free cash flow they continue to generate while they are going through this transition.

Hardware Wanes, But Authentication Remains a Vital Industry

It was not so long ago that DigiPass hardware authentication was OneSpan’s bread and butter product. In recent years though, hardware is less and less a vital part of their revenue stream.

2022

2023

2024

2025 (9 months)

Subscriptions

$47 million

$61 million

$81 million

$70 million

Maintenance

$43 million

$42 million

$38 million

$26 million

Professional Services

$7.1 million

$5.4 million

$4.4 million

$2.2 million

Hardware Products

$74 million

$76 million

$59 million

$35 million

(Source: 10-Ks from 2024, 2025 and most recent 10-Q from SEC)

While hardware isn’t totally off the revenue sheets, it went from the largest line item in OneSpan’s revenue to a very secondary line in a few short years. Indeed, the most recent quarter saw subscription revenue up to $21.1 million, the biggest line item, and hardware down to $9.7 million, less than half that.

I believe this was less of a decision by OneSpan about the nature of their product than the market telling them what kind of products they wanted, and OneSpan leaning into providing them. The good news is that while hardware authenticators have their uses, and once upon a time set OneSpan apart as an unquestioned market leader, their margins seem to be improving with the shift to more software-oriented solutions.

2024 (Q1-Q3)

2025 (Q1-Q3)

Total Revenue

$182 million

$180 million

Gross Profits

$129 million

$133 million

Gross Margins

70.9%

73.9%

Operating Income

$33 million

$36 million

Operating Margin

18.1%

20%

Net Income

$28.3 million

$29.4 million

GAAP EPS

74¢

77¢

Non-GAAP EPS

$1.07

$1.12

(source: most recent 10-Q from SEC)

On the one hand, OneSpan is not growing on a revenue basis right now, but with improvements in their margins, they actually are slowly increasing their earnings. I think it would also be a mistake to discount them as a company that is no longer growing, because we saw before that the subscription revenue actually is on the rise, and it’s just been masked by declining hardware sales over the same period.

The estimate for Q4 is that OneSpan will earn another 29¢, bringing their non-GAAP EPS to $1.41 for the year, implying a forward P/E of 7.99 in current prices. This is the first hint that we’re looking at a value stock, because the sector median is a substantially higher 23.12.

Even leaving future growth somewhat uncertain, I’m a lot more comfortable, personally, justifying paying just under 8 times forward earnings than paying huge premiums for the fashionable choices in the hopes that they deliver on future growth. That’s especially true when we have clarity on OneSpan’s management of their free cash flow.

Cash Flow, Dividends and Buybacks

This is a good time to start looking at OneSpan’s cash flow, because operating free cash flow is relatively strong, and where the money all goes in quite illuminating.

2024 (Q1-Q3)

2025 (Q1-Q3)

Operating Free Cash Flow

$43.2 million

$46.9 million

Investing Free Cash Flow

($7.3 million)

($20 million)

Financing Free Cash Flow

($2.8 million)

($26 million)

(Source: most recent 10-Q from SEC)

$46.9 million in operating FCF is a good start for OneSpan, and we can see where that virtually entirely went out as expenses elsewhere. But what does that entail?

The investing FCF breaks down as $6.0 million in CapEx and around $14 million in costs for the acquisition of businesses. In 2025 this included the acquisition of Nok Nok and roughly a 15% equity stake in ThreatFabric. We’ll be talking about acquisitions more in the next section, but that’s how it was accounted for.

The financing FCF expenses break down as $13.9 million for dividends, $4.4 million for restricted stock issuance, and $6.3 for share repurchases. In other words, most of the financing FCF went directly to the shareholders, either through dividends or buybacks.

The dividends right now are 12¢ per quarter, which is a yield of 4.26%, once again vastly better than the sector median of 1.31%. Now a 4.26% dividend by itself isn’t necessarily an income stock for many, but when you combine it with regular stock buybacks, that amounts to a company doing a very good job of returning value to shareholders.

OneSpan’s Buying Spree: Expanding Their Software Options

OneSpan’s transition from a mostly hardware company to a mostly software company may have been driven primarily by what their customers were demanding, but the company was built around hardware, and the change to a software-centric model created some holes their workflow wasn’t built to solve. OneSpan then became a buyer of competitors who added a piece to the overall puzzle for their new subscription model.

That’s the other big expense, in addition to dividends, that we’re seeing OneSpan dedicate their cash flow toward. In June, OneSpan acquired Nok Nok Labs, a passwordless authentication provider. In October they made an investment in fraud detection company ThreatFabric, taking a 15% equity stake there as well.

In January of this year, OneSpan announced an even bigger acquisition, in the former of Build38. Build38 is seen as enhancing security on the mobile stage for OneSpan’s offerings, and while the terms haven’t yet been released to the public, it is likely from the scale of Build38’s past VC equity offerings that this will be a bigger transaction than Nok Nok or the ThreatFabric equity.

How much any individual acquisition will add to the bottom line is difficult to say, as these are all small, specialized firms, and OneSpan is shaping itself as an all-in-one house with these consolidations. The goal is no real secret – it’s the return of revenue growth for OneSpan in the subscription realm. The expectation is that Nok Nok will be accretive to earnings in Q4, and Build38 opens OneSpan up to a whole new group of potential customers in finance, as Build38 secures some 250 million endpoints.

OneSpan’s Industry: Vital But Inconsistent

Online security and authentication for finance is simultaneously an extremely important industry segment and one of the most uncertain ones imaginable. Whatever else you can say, OneSpan’s customers will absolutely continue to rely on industry solutions to try to keep their transactions secure. There’s a lot of money involved here, and Internet security is indispensable because there are no shortage of groups out there trying to circumvent security and take what isn’t theirs. We hear the most about this industry when the security is tested and found wanting, so to some extent, the less we hear about security breaches, the better.

But these are ever-present threats, and where the money goes in this segment isn’t always predictable. The clearest example of this would be that OneSpan was a relatively large hardware company before, and even though they’re providing solutions to the same industry, less and less of that is hardware-centric.

The risk, then, is that OneSpan has revised itself away from being a hardware company, invested in acquiring other software companies, and the industry is apt to change its mind in the face of new threats or different business models, and what OneSpan is invested in now might not fit future market demands.

That’s not to say that another change is necessarily imminent. Indeed, the industry seems quite happy moving away from hardware and toward subscription models right now, and the extent to which that continues is the extent to which OneSpan’s investments and focus could pay off. As I said above, the less we hear, the better.

Small But Noteworthy Value

Recognizing that we need to keep an eye out for potential changes that would alter customer expectations, I’m going to initiate OneSpan as a buy. We’ve got a price/book ratio well below the sector median (1.80 vs. 3.67), a forward P/E ratio that’s lower, and a dividend yield that is much better than the average of the sector. The numbers suggest that OneSpan is rather cheap at these prices.

Cash flow is the key here, allowing OneSpan to sustain a good dividend, share repurchases, and investing in future growth. Investors should keep an eye on margins, because the transition to the subscription model seems to have helped so far, and with a track record of returning cash to shareholders, further improvements may set the stage for more buybacks. At the current multiples, that would be a worthwhile complement to the dividend, and valuable for investors intending to hold OneSpan for the long term.

data.title: W. P. Carey: 138% Coverage, 5% Yield, Strong Income Value
data.description:
W. P. Carey (WPC) completed $2.1B in acquisitions in the 2025 fiscal year and managed to outperform expectations for its 2026 adjusted funds from operations. Due to a rebound in underlying adjusted FFO, the commercial REIT bumped its dividend by a 4.5% as well and raised its dividend every single quarter in the last year. Additionally, the REIT managed to beat expectations for both the bottom and the top lines earlier this month and is on track to buy billions more of real estate in 2026. W. P. Carey is set to benefit from strong growth prospects in its core industrial and warehouse real estate portfolio and generates positive same store rental growth. Although shares recently hit a new 52 week high and are not as cheap as they were last year, I maintain a strong buy rating for W. P. Carey.

Previous rating

I rated shares of W. P. Carey a strong buy in my last coverage in July 2025 -- Ripe For A Breakout -- because I liked the reposition and of the REIT's portfolio and the strategic pivot toward industrial and warehouse assets. W. P. Carey supported its dividend with adjusted FFO in the last quarter and maintained a stable coverage profile throughout the year. I see prospects for continued growth of AFFO and the dividend, and view shares as moderately valued given the strength of the value proposition.

Strong dividend coverage, dividend growth, well-rented portfolio

W. P. Carey is a hybrid commercial property REIT with significant investments in warehouses, industrial properties, retail properties, and even some self-storage facilities. At the end of the fourth quarter, W. P. Carey owned 1,682 properties in its portfolio, showing an 8% increase in property count compared to the year-earlier period. According to the REIT's update for the fourth quarter, the real estate investment company stemmed a total acquisition volume of $2.1B in 2025, which marked a strong rebound year from 2024 which saw a major portfolio contraction due to the spin-off and sales of non-strategic office properties.

After selling off its office properties in 2023 and 2024, the real estate investment trust pivoted towards industrial and warehouse properties which today represent approximately 64% of all investments. At the end of 2023, this percentage stood at 59%. The biggest category for W. P. Carey is the industrial segment -- representing ~38% of all real estate -- and which is seeing strong demand for distribution centers, driven by the ascent of the U.S. e-Commerce industry after the COVID-19 pandemic.

One key aspect that I like about W. P. Carey is that the REIT has been fairly successful in consistently growing its same rental growth rate in its core portfolio, which together with a high occupancy rate of 98% -- reflects very strong lease fundamentals. The same store rental growth rate in Q4’25 was 2.4% (stable Q/Q) -- with about half of their leases linked to inflation.

The growth in W. P. Carey’s same store rental rate backs up the REIT’s moderate adjusted FFO growth. In the most recent quarter, Q4’25, W. P. Carey generated $281.1M in adjusted FFO, showing 5% year-over-year growth.

WPC in $'000

Q4'24

Q1'25

Q2'25

Q3'25

Q4'25

Y/Y Growth

FFO

$188,293

$219,403

$124,824

$243,164

$288,072

53%

AFFO

$267,606

$257,820

$282,670

$276,629

$281,124

5%

Source: Author

Over the course of the last year, W. P. Carey’s dividend coverage has remained fairly steady which is due to the fact that the REIT’s ~5% growth rate in adjusted funds operations per-share was mirrored in the dividend growth rate: both went up by 5% Y/Y. Over the course of the last year W. P. Carey raised its dividend four times -- each quarter by $0.01 per-share to a final rate of $0.92 per-share in Q4'25. The REIT is likely going to continue to raise its dividend by $0.01 per-share each quarter throughout 2026.

WPC

Q4'24

Q1'25

Q2'25

Q3'25

Q4'25

Y/Y Growth

AFFO

$1.21

$1.17

$1.28

$1.25

$1.27

5%

Dividend

$0.88

$0.89

$0.90

$0.91

$0.92

5%

Coverage Trend

138%

131%

142%

137%

138%

0%

Source: Author

Valuation profile

The REIT has given guidance for the 2026 fiscal year and expects adjusted FFO of $5.13-5.23 per-share which beat the average Wall Street estimate of $5.07 per-share by a decent $0.11 per-share. The guidance assumes $1.25-1.75B in investments in FY 2026 which is set to add considerably to the REIT's cash flow.

Based off of the new AFFO projection, the REIT’s management expects ~4% year-over-year growth in this important metric. At the midpoint, W. P. Carey expects $5.18 per-share in adjusted FFO which calculates to a forward price-to-AFFO ratio of 13.9X.

Key rivals in W. P. Carey's core operating segment of industrial, warehouse and retail properties are Realty Income (O) and National Retail Properties (NNN). Realty Income has not yet submitted its 2026 AFFO guidance, but the analyst consensus is $4.41-4.48 per-share which implies ~5% year-over-year growth and a forward P/AFFO ratio of 14.7X.

National Retail Properties has guided for $3.52-3.58 per-share in FY 2026 AFFO meaning the REIT sees ~3% year-over-year growth, which is slightly below W. P. Carey's guidance. The implied forward P/AFFO ratio for NNN is 12.4X, making it the cheapest choice in the industry peer group.

In my opinion, WPC could trade at a 15.0X price-to-AFFO ratio easily, considering that the REIT has returned to strong growth after its office spin-off/sale program kicked off in 2023. WPC's portfolio, AFFO, and dividend are all growing, and the portfolio itself has a strong constitution, with rising same-store rents and near-perfect occupancy rates. A 15.0X P/AFFO ratio implies a fair value of $77.70 per-share which calculates to 8% upside potential. This capital growth potential, of course, comes in addition to a well-supported 5% dividend yield.

Primary risks with W. P. Carey countering my thesis

The biggest risk for the commercial REIT is a potential slowdown in the pace of acquisitions, as they are a lever for stronger AFFO growth. From a dividend standpoint, I believe that W. P. Carey is well-positioned to support its dividend going forward and that the REIT will continue to grow its dividend by approximately $0.01 per share each quarter in 2026. What would change my mind about WPC is if the REIT saw its dividend coverage ratio fall below 120% or if it suffered a deterioration in its same-store rental growth prospects.

Final thoughts

W. P. Carey's earnings report card for the fourth quarter on February 10, 2026, showed that the REIT is growing its portfolio rapidly, via acquisitions mainly, and that it easily supports its dividend with adjusted funds from operations, leading to a secure 5% yield. W. P. Carey, given the stability of its underlying rental growth, should have no challenges in 2026 supporting its dividend with AFFO, and I see a strong year ahead for the year in terms of acquisition-driven growth. In my opinion, W. P. Carey is a rock-solid choice in the commercial REIT market with strong coverage, mid-single-digit growth in adjusted funds from operations (a cash flow metric), and shares that are not overly expensive relative to peers.

data.title: MFA Financial: A Look At Their Latest Results And Impact On Baby Bonds
data.description:
Thesis

MFA Financial (MFA) is a U.S.-based real estate investment trust. As per the Seeking Alpha landing page:

MFA Financial, Inc., together with its subsidiaries, operates as a real estate investment trust in the United States. It invests in residential mortgage securities, including non-agency mortgage-backed securities, agency MBS, and credit risk transfer securities; residential whole loans, including purchased performing loans, purchased credit deteriorated, and non-performing loans; and mortgage servicing rights related assets.

With the company just releasing its Q4 2025 results, we are going to have a closer look at the name and its baby bonds.

Company results

The company just announced its Q4 2025 financials, beating revenue figures. MFA is a hybrid mREIT, having a mixed portfolio of Agency MBS and other mortgage related securities:

The above parsing for the REIT's investment portfolio comes out to the following percentages:

  • Non-QM Loans: 43%
  • Agency MBS: 27%
  • Single-family Rental Loans: 10%
  • Legacy RPL/NPL: 8%
  • Single-family Transitional Loans: 6%
  • Other: residual

The company disclosed it added more assets in Q4, to the tune of $1.9 billion, out of which $1.2 billion were Agency MBS, while $443 million were Non-QM loans. These additions were on the back of an $814 portfolio runoff. As per the company, the average coupon on the acquired loans was 8%.

The Q4 loan portfolio metrics have remained healthy:

The 60+ day delinquency rate rose slightly to 7.1% but has remained within the historic norms for the company. The company also runs a significant amount of high coupon securities, meaning it is set to benefit from refinancings as rates move lower.

The REIT also makes the following disclosures when it comes to its Agency MBS sleeve of business:

The constant prepayment rate (CPR) has increased to 15% in December, all while Agency MBS spreads have further compressed to 100 bps. To note, the company has allocated more resources to Agency MBS and will benefit from lower Fed Funds as it uses repo structures to fund this business segment (as Fed Funds decrease, repo rates also move lower).

Maintaining book value

The company's common shares have performed fairly well in the past three years:

From a common equity standpoint, the shares are down only -8%, while the total return (i.e. including dividends) is at a large +37%. These are very robust results for a very tumultuous three years when rate volatility is considered.

Furthermore, the company has managed to maintain a fairly stable book value:

Book value per share came in at $13.2, up on the quarter and almost unchanged on the year. The steep discount of the shares versus book value has prompted management to issue more preferred shares in order to buy back common shares:

Finally, our listeners will recall that we began a program in the third quarter of 2025 to issue additional shares of our 2 preferred stock issues via an ATM and use the proceeds to repurchase our common stock at a significant discount to book. The stock buyback authorization expired at the end of last year, but our Board has reauthorized this program, and we expect that we will continue to utilize these 2 programs when the trading window opens after we file our 10-K.

So the company will continue to further dilute preferred shareholders in order to accrete book value via common share repurchases.

Liabilities and baby bonds

The REIT has an interesting structure for its liabilities:

Most of the funding is done long-term via securitized debt placement. The second largest portion is represented by mark to market funding, mainly via repo facilities on agency MBS securities. The third, and smallest piece, is the non-MTM financing via baby bonds.

There are two issuances of baby bonds for this REIT:

  • Feb 2029 8.87% Notes (MFAN) with a notional of $200 M
  • Aug 2029 9% Notes (MFAO) with a notional of $85 M

In today's article we are going to focus on the more liquid and larger MFAN securities. Additional details regarding them are as follows:

  • Maturity date: Feb 15, 2029
  • Call date: Feb 15, 2026
  • Coupon: 8.875%
  • Payment period: quarterly
  • Price: $25.27
  • YTM: 8.45%
  • YTC (1Y): 7.7%

We are calculating here a yield to call out one year from today. Rather than do brain damage by assuming the bond will be called next payment date (it won't), we are running under the assumption that the company continuing to post robust results coupled with the Fed cutting this year will result in the possibility of refinancing early next year at more attractive rates. Please note the REIT would need to pay banks 0.5% to 0.75% for a new issuance; thus, the math makes sense when they can place paper in the 7.5% - 7.8% area. That will not happen in the next few months.

From a strategic standpoint, the company has shown that it is willing to dilute preferred shareholders in order to close book value on the common stock. With the term liabilities, there is less of an incentive to do that since they have a maturity date. As the Fed cuts, the company will probably take a more overweight view on shorter-term financing (i.e. repos) in order to get cheaper funds and eventually refinance or retire more expensive debt. We are of the opinion that the smaller and more expensive MFAO will be first; thus, MFAN looks more attractive, with at least one year left outstanding.

Conclusion

MFA is a hybrid REIT focused on Non-QM loans and Agency MBS. The company had a robust Q4 2025, with a stabilizing book value and dividend coverage. The company has issued more preferred shares in 2025 to repurchase back common shares trading at a large discount to book value. Management expects this trend to continue. While most of the MFA funding is done via securitizations and repos, the company has 2 baby bonds outstanding. We favor MFAN going forward, given its lower coupon and propensity to get called. While preferred equity holders can get diluted via more issuances, it is not the case for bondholders.

data.title: Microsoft And Meta Earnings Review And Outlook
data.description:
Microsoft (MSFT) reported its second quarter fiscal 2026 earnings on Wednesday, January 28, delivering a beat on both revenue and profit versus Visible Alpha consensus expectations.

The company’s shares, however, have since fallen nearly 18%, amid concerns around signs that AI may disrupt the present software business models. In addition, the scale of Microsoft’s AI-related capex has increased to a projected $106.9 billion for FY 2026.

Meta Platforms (META) reported its fourth quarter fiscal 2025 earnings on the same day, also surpassing consensus expectations. While the company’s stock jumped in after-hours trading after the earnings release, Meta shares have since traded lower.

Microsoft: Q2 2025 review and outlook

Microsoft posted revenue of $81.3 billion in Q2, up 16.7% year-on-year, slowing slightly from 18.4% growth in Q1 but still ahead of consensus expectations of $80.3 billion.

Earnings also came in well above expectations, with diluted EPS of $5.16, topping the $3.87 analysts had anticipated, due to the $10 billion gain from OpenAI.

Microsoft’s Productivity and Business Processes segment, which includes revenue from Microsoft 365 Commercial and Consumer Cloud, generated $34.1 billion in revenue, exceeding consensus forecasts of $33.5 billion.

Meanwhile, Intelligent Cloud, home to Azure and server products, delivered revenue of $32.9 billion, ahead of estimates of $32.4 billion.

Microsoft’s investment cycle remains a central focus for investors. Capital expenditure surged to $37.5 billion in Q2, up from $22.6 billion a year earlier and above consensus expectations of $34.3 billion.

Consensus revisions following the quarter were modest at the headline level but more meaningful within segment detail. Total revenue expectations for fiscal 3Q 2026 have been revised only marginally, from $81.39 billion to $81.41 billion.

Revisions are strongest in Microsoft’s key growth pillars:

  • Productivity and Business Processes expectations raised +1.5% vs. preQ
  • Intelligent Cloud raised +1.1%, with Azure revenue lifted +1.4%, with implied growth moving up to 40%

Offsetting these increases were sharp downward revisions in consumer segments:

  • Bing/More Personal Computing expectations cut -6.5%
  • Gaming revised down -5.7%
  • Bing and advertising revised down -2.1%

Despite Q2 margin upside, analyst expectations of Q3 operating income have edged down slightly (-0.6% vs. preQ), implying expectations for higher expenses. Still, EPS estimates have moved higher by +2.2%.

Meta Platforms: Q4 2025 review and outlook

Meta delivered strong Q4 2025 earnings, with results coming in ahead of Visible Alpha consensus expectations across revenue, profitability, and earnings per share.

The quarter reinforced continued momentum in the company’s advertising business. The forward revisions pointed to a solid outlook heading into 2026. However, DAU trends were mixed in Q4 2025 and for Q1 2026.CapEx expectations continued to rise sharply, with FY 2026 revised up significantly after the Q4 2025 earnings release.

Meta reported revenues of $59.9 billion, ahead of Visible Alpha consensus expectations of $58.4 billion. The company reported diluted EPS of $8.88, which also surpassed Visible Alpha expectations of $8.24.

The outperformance was largely driven by the Family of Apps segment, which generated $58.9 billion in Q4, vs. consensus expectations of $57.5 billion.

Reality Labs revenue came in slightly ahead of expectations but remains a smaller contributor with less visibility relative to the core advertising engine. Advertising revenue grew 24.3% year-on-year in Q4 to $58.1 billion, ahead of the $56.8 billion expected preQ.

Daily active user (DAU) trends were slightly below expectations:

  • US & Canada DAUs: 210 million vs. 211 million expected (slight miss)
  • Europe DAUs: 315 million vs. 317 million expected (slight miss)
  • Asia DAUs: 970 million vs. 981 million expected (largest shortfall)

One of the most notable takeaways post-earnings for Meta has been the sharp upward revisions we’re seeing in analyst expectations for early 2026.

For Q1 2026, consensus revenue estimates have been revised materially higher:

  • Revenue expectation raised from $51.3 billion to $55.5 billion
  • EPS estimates revised higher by 6.1%, signaling continued earnings strength near term
  • Q1 capex revised up 10.9%

Full-year expectations have also moved up:

  • FY2026 revenue revised from $236.7 billion to $250.7 billion
  • Full-year EPS revisions have been modest (+0.5%), suggesting that rising costs may offset some of the topline upside longer term
  • FY2026 capex revised up 13.4%

Overall, Meta exited 2025 with solid fundamentals, but there seems to be an increasing focus on whether elevated CapEx would translate into sustained monetization and margin durability through 2026.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Treasury Yields Snapshot: February 20, 2026
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The yield on the 10-year note finished February 20, 2026, at 4.08%. Meanwhile, the 2-year note ended at 3.48%, and the 30-year note ended at 4.72%.

The chart below overlays the daily performance of several Treasury bonds, starting from the pre-recession equity market peaks, along with the Federal Funds Rate (FFR) since 2007.

This next table shows the highs and lows of yields and the Federal Funds Rate since 2007.

A Long-Term Look at the 10-Year Treasury Yield

Here is a long-term view of the 10-year yield starting in 1965, well before the 1973 oil embargo that triggered the era of 'stagflation' (economic stagnation coupled with inflation)

Inverted Yield Curve

An inverted yield curve is when longer-term Treasury yields are lower than their shorter-term counterparts. The next chart displays the latest 10-2 spread. Typically, the spread turns negative for a period before rising again prior to recessions, as illustrated in the four recessions shown on this chart. For this reason, the 10-2 spread is widely considered a reliable leading indicator for recessions. The lead time between a negative spread and the onset of a recession varies, with recessions beginning anywhere from 18 to 92 weeks after the spread goes negative.

One false positive is seen in 1998, where the spread briefly went negative without leading to a recession. In the case of the 2009 recession, the spread went negative multiple times before rising again. Most recently, the spread was continuously negative from July 5, 2022, to August 26, 2024. The last time the spread was negative was on September 5, 2024.

If we consider the first negative spread date as the starting point, the average lead time to a recession is 48 weeks, or about eleven months. If we instead use the last positive spread date before a recession, the average lead time is 18.5 weeks, or about 4.25 months.

For another perspective on the yield curve, the 10-3mo spread below uses an even shorter-term maturity. The lead time to recessions based on this spread (after it turns negative) ranges from 34 to 69 weeks. Like the 10-2 spread, we see the same false positive in 1998, as well as multiple instances of the spread turning negative before rising again ahead of the 2009 recession. Recently, the spread was negative from October 25, 2022, to December 12, 2024. Since February 26th, the spread has swung back and forth from positive to negative territory.

If we consider the first negative spread date as the starting point, the average lead time to a recession is 48 weeks, or about eleven months. If we instead use the last positive spread date after the spread had been negative, the average lead time is 13 weeks, or about three months.

The 30-Year Fixed Rate Mortgage

The Federal Funds Rate influences the cost of borrowing for banks. When the Fed increases this rate, banks often raise their lending rates, which can impact mortgage rates, among other things. Therefore, a rising FFR generally leads to higher mortgage rates, and vice versa. However, this was not the case recently when the Fed began their rate-cutting cycle in September 2024 and mortgage rates moved in the opposite direction. With that said, mortgage rates have been on the decline as of late, following a similar fashion to the FFR. The latest Freddie Mac Weekly Primary Mortgage Market Survey put the 30-year fixed rate at 6.01%, its lowest level since 2022.

Now let's see the 10-year against the S&P 500 with some notes on Federal Reserve intervention. Fed policy has been a major influence on market behavior.

For a long-term view of weekly Treasury yields, also focusing on the 10-year, see our latest Treasury Yields in Perspective update.

ETFs associated with Treasuries include: Vanguard 0-3 Month Treasury Bill ETF (VBIL), Vanguard Intermediate-Term Treasury ETF (VGIT), and Vanguard Long-Term Treasury ETF (VGLT).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Monster Beverage: I Want To Own It But Can't At This Price
data.description:
Monster Beverage (MNST) is one of the companies I have been watching from afar for some time now, and I regret not having initiated a position. I like the product, and I like the company even more, not to mention that I strongly believe in the energy drink market and the compounding effect this could have on Monster's top line and EPS in the coming years.

But as great as everything looks, I still don't think it's good enough to buy, nor to hold, as I think the margin of safety is so narrow that it could even trigger a significant drawdown if the market enters a period of “bad mood” or even if Monster slightly fails to deliver on some of its estimates.

This was precisely one of the topics of the last podcast I participated in with Rena Sherbill, Jack Bowman, and Julia Ostian (check it here). Monster wasn't even mentioned, but we talked about how buying companies you personally like isn't always a good idea, and Julia also commented on investors who become passionate about companies.

What I mean here is that these factors of really liking a company or a product end up taking little account of points such as valuation, which in Monster's case is certainly one of the pillars. Compounding may even occur on the top line, but for this to reach the shareholder, the price must necessarily be good enough (unfortunately, not the case here).

Therefore, I begin my coverage of MNST with a sell rating.

Secular Trends For Monster

I'll start talking about Monster's investment case with an anecdote. Monster has been around for a long time, but the brand has become a cultural phenomenon in recent years. And if we trace a timeline, with each passing year I drank more Monster. Actually, I was drinking more energy drinks in general, but Monster was my personal choice.

I don't know exactly how often I consume it, but I definitely consume way more Monster than alcohol (I drink very little). I'm saying this because I don't think it's about me, I'm convinced it's a generational shift, almost like a "secular" trend.

Just look at this data: young people are drinking less and less alcohol, as this Time article shows. It's obvious to me that Monster is one of the big winners of this trend. A Mintel survey shows that Gen Z is shifting its consumption to energy drinks.

Then we also have the caffeine factor. Monster is a drink that you usually take to stay more focused, active, go to the gym, and so on. This also applies to the coffee industry.

Although I don't think younger generations dislike coffee, there are already some reports that show that the way they consume it is different (check it here). And in my view, energy drinks end up coming in as an alternative. Don't want to drink black coffee or go to Starbucks (SBUX) to have a prepared coffee (and still pay a high price, relatively)? Okay, buy a Monster at 7-Eleven then.

There are forecasts that point to a ~9% growth per year until 2030, but I won't be surprised if Monster beats this trend.

Monster: Capturing All That and Then Some More

And at first glance, Monster seems like the perfect company for you to invest in and capture all that growth. Not only because it is one of the leading brands, but because its execution is excellent. The Q3 earnings showed this: the company managed to grow its consolidated top line by 16% YoY, and if we remove the FX impact, the energy drinks segment grew by 17.7% YoY.

In addition, international expansion is in full swing, growing 23.3% excluding FX effects.

Furthermore, it seems to me that Monster is not only capturing the industry segment, but in a way, creating this industry growth, creating demand through its marketing, introducing new flavors, and managing to make a drink that is “engaging.”

Another crucial point here, and one of the reasons I mentioned that Monster seems “perfect” to ride this trend, is that they are still gaining efficiency. The gross margin went from 53% to almost 56%. So I thought, “it may have been some commodity tailwind,” but no, aluminum costs increased, and the explanation was better pricing, supply chain, and better revenue mix. Other than that, operating expenses went from 27.6% of sales to 25%, meaning they are growing at a much slower pace than revenue.

In fact, this is one of the things that could change my stance on MNST stock. Neither I nor the market expects this momentum to continue for long. For Q4 earnings, which are due next week, the market expects revenue of ~$2.04 billion, or YoY growth of around 13%, and for the 2026 consolidated results, the market already sees figures more in line with historical data and the industry, with top line growth of ~10% or 9%.

So if Monster shows that it can actually maintain growth closer to the low teens for the next few years, that already makes a big difference in compounding. Not to mention that if Monster continues to deliver this operating leverage, it also generates an explosive effect on the bottom line.

So, Why Is MNST A Sell?

The only reason Monster is a sell is its valuation right now. In my base scenario (which already incorporates industry growth and a certain margin gain), MNST remains an overvalued stock in a few years. And that hinders compounding.

For the top line, I assumed growth slightly above 10%. I.e., a premium relative to industry growth, as I believe Monster should be able to exploit new optionalities and increase prices in a more sustainable way than the energy drink market average. Other than that, my expectations for margin expansion are not so optimistic, going from ~23.5% in 2025 to 25% in 2030, which is super achievable.

But note that in this base scenario, it is still difficult to see value for MNST stock. We are talking about waiting 5 years, and the P/E will still be 23.5x. In other words, even if you consider a fair forward P/E of ~30x, we are talking about waiting until 2029 to have an upside of only 27%.

And note that this is a scenario between the base and the optimistic. Let's say that for some reason, whether due to some macroeconomic factor or because the market now has worse prospects for Monster, the “fair P/E” falls to 20x, a multiple closer to mature compounders such as Coca-Cola (KO). We are talking about a 38% drawdown based on estimated net income for 2027.

In other words, it is a valuation that allows little upside if Monster continues to perform well, but if something goes wrong, the drawdown could be significant. It is a bad asymmetry. In addition, let's consider that even with all this quality, Monster is not a company free of uncertainties. Trends can change, brand perception can fall if a marketing campaign is not very well executed, and competition with new players such as Celsius (CELH) may intensify even further.

Bottom Line

Monster is still a company I admire, but the stock is a no-go for me. That's at current prices, because I'm still going to keep MNST on my radar and monitor earnings and any dips the stock may have, to perhaps add it to the portfolio at some point.

My rating for MNST is a sell for now.

data.title: Pfizer: A Risky 6.3% Yield For Income-Oriented Investors
data.description:
While I usually focus my research on the financial sector, for my personal retirement portfolio, I also look for income opportunities across other sectors to have a more balanced and diversified stock portfolio. In the past, I have analyzed some companies in the chemicals sector as they offer very high-dividend yields, such as LyondellBasell (LYB) which I’ve covered some months ago but decided to move on from because I don’t think its dividend is sustainable over the long term.

Another stock that has grabbed my attention lately has been Pfizer (PFE), as this blue chip offers a very high-dividend yield, which is not usual for large companies. Indeed, Pfizer’s current dividend yield is about 6.3%, much higher than other big pharma companies, such as Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), Eli Lilly (LLY), or Novartis (NVS), which offer yields between 0.7%-2.8%. This means Pfizer’s income appeal seems to be quite good within the pharma industry, but as usual with high-dividend yielders, this can be both a signal that its shares are undervalued or that there are some sustainability issues about its dividend.

Dividend Sustainability

As can be seen from the next graph, Pfizer’s dividend history is quite good, as the company has paid dividends consecutively for the past 36 years and has delivered a growing dividend for the past 15 years, making its current high dividend yield the result of a lower share price in recent years. Indeed, since its peak in 2021, its share price is down by more than half and is trading nowadays at a price-to-earnings multiple of less than 10x, being at a significant discount to its peer group.

This seems to be a sign of undervaluation, but investors should take into account that Pfizer benefitted greatly from the COVID-19 vaccine and antiviral, reaching combined peak revenues of about $57 billion in 2022. This pushed its valuation to much higher levels, which normalized in the following years as the pandemic phase ended and demand for its products declined accordingly. Indeed, looking at its financial figures over the past few years, Pfizer reported peak revenues of $101 billion in 2022, way higher than its annual revenues before the pandemic ($41 billion in 2019 and $41.6 billion in 2020). Its bottom line was also much higher in 2022 (above $31 billion) compared to 2019 ($16 billion) and 2020 ($9 billion). This shows how big the impact of COVID-19 was in its financial figures, as Pfizer together with its vaccine partner BioNTech (BNTX), was one of the leading providers of COVID-19 related treatments.

However, this was a temporary boost to its revenues and earnings, and, naturally, as demand for COVID-19 treatments declined over the past couple of years, Pfizer’s financial figures returned to levels closer to what it reported before the pandemic.

More recently, Pfizer reported revenues of $62.6 billion in 2025, down by 2% YoY, but up by 6% from the previous year when excluding Paxlovid and Comirnaty (COVID-19 products), showing that its top line is now stabilizing and may reverse its declining trend ahead. Its reported net income amounted to nearly $7.8 billion, down by 3% YoY, while its adjusted net income was $18.4 billion (+4% YoY).

The difference between the two metrics is mainly related to restructuring costs and amortization of intangible assets, which amounted to more than $6.3 billion in the year, plus other significant items that the company excludes to arrive at adjusted earnings. Generally speaking, I prefer to analyze earnings based on GAAP metrics rather than adjusted ones, as management can be quite aggressive in their ‘adjustments’ and several items seem to be recurring in its financial figures and should not be treated as one-off items in my opinion.

For example, amortization of intangible assets amounted to $4.9 billion in 2025, $3.3 billion in 2024, $3 billion in 2022, $1.7 billion in 2019, $2.8 billion in 2018, and some smaller values in other years. As shown in the next graph, this seems to be recurring over the years and therefore I think investors should be conservative and include this type of expense in the company’s ‘normal’ earnings, unless Pfizer stops doing asset write-downs with such frequency over the next few years.

Taking this background into account, to analyze its dividend sustainability, I prefer to use its reported GAAP earnings per share, which amounted to $1.36 in 2025, down 3% YoY. Considering that Pfizer’s current quarterly dividend is $0.43 per share, this leads to an annual dividend of $1.72 per share. This means Pfizer’s payout ratio based on reported earnings is 126%, not a good sign for its dividend sustainability, as the dividend is not covered by reported earnings.

Having said that, this does not necessarily mean its dividend is not sustainable, as the company is more focused on adjusted EPS, which was $3.22 per share in 2025. This leads to a dividend payout ratio of 53%, based on adjusted earnings, which is a very acceptable level for a mature company like Pfizer.

More important than earnings to analyze the dividend sustainability is the cash flow coverage, which shows a stretched coverage in recent quarters. Pfizer’s annual dividend payments amounted to about $9.7 billion in 2025, which were barely covered by expected free cash flow of around $10.5 billion in the year. Investors should note that Pfizer has not yet submitted its 10-K report to the SEC, and therefore these should be treated as ‘preliminary’ figures. Nevertheless, its dividend seems to be covered by its cash flow generation, as the payout ratio is 92%, but this is a high level and may not be sustainable over the long term.

Regarding its balance sheet, Pfizer has an acceptable leverage position, but following the acquisition of Metsera back in November, its gross leverage is expected to have increased to about 2.7x at the end of 2025.

This transaction was funded by a mix of cash and new long-term debt, putting further pressure on the company’s balance sheet, pushing its net leverage ratio to a level closer to 1.9x at the end of 2025 based on $5 billion of new debt issued to finance this acquisition. While I see this leverage profile as still being acceptable, investors should note that the company was downgraded by credit agencies in the past following the Seagen acquisition due to increased leverage, and I think Pfizer does not have much room to increase leverage further if it wants to maintain a strong credit rating in the near future.

Moreover, its financial position was increased by more than desired from the Metsera acquisition, as Pfizer had to eventually pay more than initially expected due to competition from Novo Nordisk (NVO), being an acquisition that is expected to be financially dilutive over the next few years, as Metsera is a biopharmaceutical company with several products in late-stage critical trials but does not generate any revenue yet.

Paying about $7 billion in cash (plus future payouts based on specific product milestones) for a company that does not generate revenue may seem excessive, but this seems to be the only way for Pfizer to potentially enter the obesity market over the coming years and be competitive against Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly, as its own drugs in the pipeline were struggling in clinical trials. Thus, only time will tell if this acquisition was worth it or not for Pfizer, as the company desperately needs new revenue streams ahead that can boost its growth prospects.

This happens because Pfizer is facing a patent cliff that is expected to put significant pressure on revenues, earnings, and cash flow over the next three years. There are several important products that will end patents in the near term, with the company expecting about $1.5 billion of lost revenue from these products in 2026. This includes products such as Eliquis, Xeljanz, among others, in the coming years, which are expected to face competition from generic drugs ahead and report much lower revenue in the future. Pfizer has guided for a loss-of-exclusivity (LOE) impact at $17-18 billion per year by 2028, explaining to some extent why Pfizer was so desperate to bid for Metsera, as the company clearly needs new products to offset the patent cliff over the coming years.

Beyond acquisitions, the company also has its own pipeline of new drugs under development, with the company investing more than $10 billion per year in research & development, which is critical for Pfizer to offset lower revenues from LOE products over the coming years.

Given this background, it’s not surprising that Pfizer’s guidance for 2026 is to report annual revenues between $59.5-$62.5 billion, representing a decline of close to 5% at the bottom of its range. This means its top line momentum is not expected to recover much over the next few quarters, a view that seems to be shared by the street, as revenue estimates for 2026 are around $61 billion and should continue to decline annually over the following years to about $52 billion by 2030, according to analysts’ estimates.

Assuming this trend as the ‘base case scenario’, Pfizer’s declining revenues are clearly negative for its dividend sustainability, something that the company aims to offset by getting approval for new drugs and from its cost savings initiatives. The company launched a cost savings program back in 2023, at an initial level of $3.5 billion, that was increased several times throughout the past years to a level above $7 billion from 2023 to 2027.

While this is important to protect business margins and offset lower revenues, earnings, and cash flow generation, it doesn’t seem to be enough to protect its dividend from the patent cliff ahead and probably its dividend distributions won’t be covered by cash flows by 2027 or 2028, depending on how much revenue it loses from key LOE products.

While I think the dividend is covered over the next three to four quarters and the company seems committed to maintaining its dividend in the near future, if its financial figures maintain a downward trajectory as currently expected, this will put pressure on its financial leverage and, eventually, Pfizer may need to save cash and do a dividend cut. I think a 20-30% dividend cut may be enough for the company to reduce organically its indebtedness and maintain a sustainable dividend, but successful new drugs from its pipeline and new revenue streams are clearly necessary for Pfizer to have a sustainable dividend over the medium to long term.

Conclusion

Pfizer is currently offering a high-dividend yield and a relatively undemanding valuation, but this seems to be justified by the company’s relatively weak growth prospects as the patent cliff is expected to impact significantly its financial performance over the next three years. While its dividend is currently covered by cash flows and Pfizer likely want to avoid a cut ahead, this may be difficult to do during the next couple of years if the company doesn’t find new revenue streams.

Thus, I think Pfizer’s dividend is attractive for income-oriented investors that are willing to take the risk and wait for the company to have new drugs approved by regulators both in the U.S. and abroad, while for investors who want to sleep well at night this is in my opinion a stock to avoid as the risk of a dividend cut should not be overlooked over the next few years.

data.title: Replimune: Multiple Shots On Goal But A High Risk Binary Approaches
data.description:
Replimune (REPL) has two oncolytic virus therapies in clinical development, RP1 and RP2, with RP1 already under review for potential marketing approval in the US. The company previously received a complete response letter (CRL) when it submitted RP1 for approval in melanoma, but soon resubmitted its application, which could be approved or rejected come April 10, 2026. Beyond that, RP1 is in additional trials, as is RP2, which provides the company with multiple shots on goal. This article takes a look at catalysts ahead for REPL, and the recent financial update.

RP1 under review again

In July 2025, REPL announced it had received a CRL for its biologics license application (BLA) for RP1 (vusolimogene oderparepvec) plus nivolumab in advanced melanoma patients who were PD-1 experienced. The FDA at that time didn't seem happy with the IGNYTE trial, despite its 32.9% objective response rate, median duration of response of 33.7 months, and 2-year survival of 63.3%. For example, the FDA noted the trial couldn't be interpreted due to heterogeneity of the patient population. Following a type A meeting in September, the company resubmitted its BLA in October, with that submission being accepted and considered to be a complete response to the CRL.

During the past few months, Replimune has been working to address agency feedback. Additional information, data and analyses were included in the resubmission which will be part of the BLA review. The FDA indicated this resubmission is considered to be a complete response to the complete response letter received in July 2025.

October 20, 2025, press release from REPL.

The current Prescription Drug User Fee Act (PDUFA) target action date for the BLA is April 10, 2026. It isn't clear if the FDA will approve the BLA this time around. At the recent JP Morgan Healthcare Conference, the analyst asked if there had been new staff members involved in the review of REPL's BLA. Some new staff involved wouldn't necessarily mean that another CRL is incoming, but it could be viewed as creating additional uncertainty.

Anupam Rama, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division

Okay. And I just want to kind of confirm that your review team kind of at the oncology division, CBER, that's not seen meaningful turnover in terms of team leads and things of that nature?

Emily Hill, Chief Financial Officer, REPL

It's hard to know because as I stated, we don't have interactions and meetings where you can see everyone around the table. We do believe there are some new faces based on the follow-up questions from our prior BLA, but we know our project manager has remained the same.

Sushil Patel, CEO & Director, REPL

Yes. Just so we know in the Type A meeting, we did have some new attendees, new people, including OCE representatives at that meeting, and we believe they're probably now also involved in the ongoing review and information requests that we're getting.

JP Morgan Healthcare Conference, January 2026.

REPL does have other shots on goal with RP1 if the FDA remains unsatisfied. For example, RP1 can be used as monotherapy in patients where an anti-PD-1 is not appropriate, such as those who have had an organ transplant. For example, REPL's ARTACUS trial looks at RP1 monotherapy in cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (CSCC), with recent data including a 34.6% objective response rate (9/36 patients) in locally advanced CSCC. As such, in a hypothetical situation where REPL's BLA is rejected again, the company can still continue developing RP1 in other indications while it considers its options of submitting again right away, or waiting for the confirmatory IGNYTE-3 trial to reach an interim.

Regarding IGNYTE-3, that trial compares RP1 plus nivolumab to physicians choice in melanoma patients who have had an anti-PD-1 and an anti-CTLA4 (if eligible). The current timeline, however, only has an interim analysis set for H2'27.

REPL notes that enrollment in the trial wasn't adversely impacted by the CRL, and so I think the company has a good chance of remaining on track with that trial.

RP2 is also in the clinic

As for RP2, the company's other oncolytic virus therapy in the clinic, the company notes the REVEAL study in metastatic uveal melanoma has already enrolled 50 patients (slide 27, January presentation). Although the planned enrollment in REVEAL is 280 patients, the company is still expecting to report data from 90 patients in Q1'27, which actually puts a readout from that trial ahead of the IGNYTE-3 interim.

Beyond REVEAL, the company is also enrolling patients in a trial of RP2 with atezolizumab/bevacizumab in hepatocellular carcinoma, and RP2 with durvalumab in biliary tract cancer. Preliminary data from the hepatocellular carcinoma cohort are expected in Q4'26, which provides a 2026 clinical readout for the company in a non skin-cancer indication.

Financial Overview

REPL finished their fiscal Q3'26 (which ended on December 31, 2025) with $269.1M in cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments. For Q3'26, REPL reported R&D expenses of $53.1M, SG&A expenses of $18.7M, and a net loss of $70.9M. REPL used its at-the-market sales agreement to raise an additional $20.8M in January 2026, which should also be considered. Further, in January 2026, REPL made a third amendment to its Hercules loan agreement that saw it draw a further $35M and move repayment of the debt (principal balance now approximately $82M) into 2027 instead of 2026. As a result, we might consider pro forma cash to be $324.9M and long-term debt to be ~$82M.

REPL's net cash used in operating activities was $224.2M for the nine-month period ending December 31, 2025, meaning at the current rate REPL would have runway for about 13 months, or until Q1'27. Indeed, REPL believes it has extended its runway into late Q1'27. The above calculation doesn't consider the company's debt, but that isn't due until the maturity date of October 1, 2027. Further, if REPL receives FDA approval prior to that date, the amortization and maturity date on its Hercules loan shall instead be October 1, 2029. Also, REPL could continue to use its at-the-market program to raise additional funds prior to the PDUFA date of April 10. REPL had $89.3M left under the ATM program at the end of 2025, and subtracting the $20.8M raised in January 2026, there would be approximately ~$68M worth of stock sales remaining. REPL might try to further boost its cash balance heading into the PDUFA date, to be prepared for the potential scenario where the company receives another CRL. Another $68M would cover about 80% of the company's long-term debt, so it would certainly help REPL prepare for the worst.

REPL trades with a market cap of $664M ($8.04 per share).

Risks

Although this article isn't advocating for a long position in REPL, given the hold rating, a few risks of a long position will be considered here.

Firstly, REPL could receive another complete response letter. The fact that the FDA soon accepted a resubmission of the company's BLA doesn't guarantee approval. If REPL's BLA is rejected again, the fall in the stock could be particularly pronounced, especially since concerns might arise about the company's ability to run its trials and repay its debt.

Further, REPL traded down about 20% in January, part of which could relate to the company using its ATM program to raise cash. If the company continues to use the program heading towards the April 10, 2026, PDUFA goal date, then the stock could trade down rather than run up into that catalyst.

Lastly, REPL could report delays or provide disappointing updates on enrollment rates or results from its trials. IGNYTE-3 had already enrolled over 60 patients out of a planned 400 at the time of the JPMorgan Healthcare Conference in mid-January, but if enrollment lags, the stock could fall.

Conclusions

REPL has catalysts ahead outside of its upcoming PDUFA date in April. Initial data from a trial of RP2 with atezolizumab/bevacizumab in hepatocellular Q4'26 could demonstrate the value of the company's oncolytic therapy outside of skin cancer. The company also has plenty of shots on goal in various skin cancers with RP1 both as monotherapy and as a combination, although a readout from the confirmatory trial IGNYTE-3 might not arrive until H2'27 or later. I believe the company produced compelling data from the IGNYTE study, and I hope the company has addressed the FDA's concerns with its resubmission, but I find it hard to bet on, and the company only has about 13 months of cash runway. I also believe the company will likely use its ATM program near-term to raise funds in case its BLA is not approved, which could hurt the stock near-term. I don't view the company as a good short, however, because the BLA could be approved early, for all we know, and trying to short what could be a modest pullback due to ATM usage might be high risk, low reward. Considering these factors, I currently rate REPL a hold.

data.title: Western Midstream: Still A High Yielder But No Longer A High Grower (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Slowing Growth

Western Midstream Partners, LP (WES) partnership units have had an impressive last 5 years, justifying the Buy rating I have maintained for most of that time frame. From lows below $3 during the 2020 crash, unit prices have improved to above $40. The quarterly distribution, which was cut in half to $0.311, has nearly tripled to $0.91 in 2025. Total return over the past 5 years has been over 290%, far exceeding the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP) or the S&P 500 index fund (SPY).

WES first got its balance sheet in order, paring down leverage and funding organic growth out of free cash flow. Over time, WES also added some targeted acquisitions, such as Meritage Midstream Partners in Wyoming's Powder River Basin for cash in 2023. More recently, WES did a bigger M&A deal, adding to its water handling business with the purchase of Aris Water Solutions for a mix of cash and stock. WES was later able to offset some of this equity issuance by redeeming some of its sponsor Occidental's (OXY) units in exchange for switching to a fixed-fee contract from a cost-of-service model. This transaction took OXY's ownership of WES down to 39.5% of the limited partner units. This increased M&A activity could be a sign of slowing organic growth ahead.

In 2025, natural gas throughput was still up by 4%, thanks to 9% gains in the Delaware Basin, offset by declines elsewhere. WES is still investing in gas processing capacity in the Delaware, with its North Loving Phase 1 starting up in 2025 and Phase 2 under construction with a completion date of Q1 2027. Crude and Natural Gas Liquids (NGLs) throughput was up only 1% for the partnership, with 6% gains in the Delaware and declines elsewhere. Water throughput grew 7% organically but 40% overall, including just 2.5 months contribution from Aris.

For 2026, WES is more conservative on growth assumptions, predicting flat throughput on natural gas, a low to mid-single digit decrease in crude and NGLs, and an 80% increase in water due to Aris. On the earnings call, management signaled a pullback in upstream activity:

based on recent discussions with our producing customers and taking into account their updated forecast, it has become clear that many of our producers will reduce previously expected activity levels on acreage that we service, including portions of the Delaware Basin.

Financially, the partnership is predicting EBITDA of $2.5 - $2.7 billion, a 4.8% increase at the midpoint, but distribution growth of only 2.2%, to $0.93 quarterly.

While that is still an attractive 9% yield, there are some warning signals we need to look for to make sure it is sustainable. First, it is important that WES not cut corners on maintenance and safety to reduce costs. Second, we want to make sure WES maintains its capital discipline and does not overleverage itself again. Finally, the partnership should only pursue accretive M&A and avoid deals that grow the company without increasing earnings per unit. If WES can avoid these risky behaviors, the 9% yield looks safe even though growth is slowing.

Safety First, Capital Discipline A Close Second

WES has already cut operating costs in 2025. Some of this involves optimizing rental equipment, contract workers, and supply chain sourcing; however, they also mention "Asset maintenance programs and schedules".

With any energy company, cutting maintenance could raise the risk of injuries or releases in the future. I am not saying it automatically does or that WES is doing anything less safe. With proper Management of Change processes to consider and avoid what could go wrong, safe cost cutting is possible. Still, investors should be on the lookout for signs of declining safety performance. WES does not regularly discuss these metrics in quarterly releases, but some are included in the 10-K, where they make up part of the formula to determine executive bonuses.

For 2025, the Total Recordable Injury Rate was 0.52, significantly exceeding the target of 0.38.

(TRIR includes injuries or illnesses that result in any of the following: days away from work, restricted work or transfer to another job, medical treatment beyond first aid, loss of consciousness, or death.)

Fortunately, the Serious Injury Frequency (fatality or permanent, life-altering impairment) was zero in 2025, but it is important to understand and fix the root causes of the less severe injuries before they result in more serious ones.

On the positive side, system operability was 99.4%, exceeding the 98% target, and Release Intensity (volumes released divided by throughput) was less than half of the target.

One yellow flag on capital discipline is the return of the Distributable Cash Flow metric, as opposed to Free Cash Flow, which has been the main focus since 2020. DCF is basically operating cash flow minus maintenance capex, but not growth capex. The partnership could fund growth capex by issuing stock or debt without much of an impact on DCF. This is not an immediate problem, as WES has pared down its net debt/EBITDA leverage to around 3, compared to above 5 in 2019. The partnership currently has an investment grade debt rating. Also, under the new DCF target, distribution coverage ratio is about 1.3, so the distribution is adequately covered.

With manageable debt levels and an investment-grade rating, it is reasonable to relax capital discipline a little to pursue high return projects or acquisitions, but with greater flexibility to do deals, the risk of overpaying or overestimating the synergies also increases.

This risk was highlighted with rumors of WES looking to buy Kinetik Holdings (KNTK). At a $7.2 billion market cap plus $4 billion of debt, Kinetik's enterprise value is nearly half that of WES. Any deal would probably involve considerable unit issuance (diluting existing holders) or added debt (resulting in higher leverage and interest expense).

Kinetik has gathering and processing facilities both north and south of WES's main footprint in the Delaware Basin as well as shares in long-haul pipelines to natural gas hubs in East Texas.

Clearly some synergies could be achieved in a deal. Kinetik's ownership of a pipeline out of the Waha hub could also be a focus of future investment, as takeaway capacity limits have resulted in low prices at Waha, contributing to the upstream production cutbacks WES is now seeing with its current customers.

On the earnings call, the WES CEO stressed that WES would only deploy capital to sustain or grow the distribution but also stated an openness to bolt-on M&A with complementary assets and synergies. So, it does not sound like a Kinetik deal is fully ruled out but would have to be at the right price.

As I said in our -- in the prepared remarks, our strategy is unchanged, and that goes for the way we think about M&A. So again, our capital deployment strategy is clear and it's tight. We only deploy capital, whether it's organic or inorganic to sustain or grow the distribution. We have demonstrated that discipline clearly last year. It is unchanged going forward. So I'm a little annoyed that people seem to be questioning that a little bit by virtue of what's happening in the marketplace.

But second, the way we think about M&A, again, is really our preference is bolt-on M&A where we have opportunities for synergies. So it fits with our assets, our geographies. We have some way a reason and competency for owning the asset. So that's unchanged. And especially as a relatively new CEO, I guess I'm kind of popular. We have had -- I've met every CEO, private and public in this space. I'm pretty sure I haven't missed anybody.

Earnings Model

Going forward, I am assuming 4% EBITDA growth and $0.02 quarterly distribution growth each year through 2029. This allows for $800 million of capex in 2027, growing by $25 million per year thereafter. Note that the 2027 capex estimate is a reduction from $925 million expected in 2026, with North Loving 2 and the Pathfinder water pipeline still under construction.

With these assumptions, the distributions are covered, but there is not a huge amount of free cash flow left over for M&A, buybacks, or faster distribution growth without dipping into the existing cash balance. Any major expansions or M&A would likely require debt or stock issuance. That is not necessarily bad. Companies like Enbridge (ENB) finance growth this way, although they always make sure new capacity is supported by firm volume commitments with take-or-pay contracts. Even so, Enbridge recently has demonstrated only slightly higher dividend growth than what WES is predicting going forward. Added interest expense or unit dilution takes a big bite out of the added EBITDA from these deals, resulting in less EPS accretion.

If WES sticks by its commitment to only do deals that grow the distribution per unit and do not overestimate the synergies, it will be fine, but distribution growth will probably remain low.

Conclusion

Organic growth of Western Midstream Partners has slowed considerably in 2026 as producers cut back activity in the face of lower prices. Looking forward, organic EBITDA growth looks limited to 4% and distribution growth a little over 2%. WES is looking at cost cuts to enhance income but must be careful to not compromise on safety. Inorganic growth through M&A can also be tempting but must be accretive to EPS, considering any unit dilution or added leverage and interest expense.

However, even with limited organic growth and no inorganic growth, the 9% yielding distribution looks adequately covered and able to go up by around 2% annually. This is fine for income investors but less attractive to investors looking for growth. As a result, I am changing my rating on WES to Hold.

data.title: Klarna: Buy The Dip In Oversold Territory As Fair Financing Takes Off
data.description:
If there is one trend that has shaped the stock market completely in 2026, it's investors' risk-off attitude toward most growth stocks. The S&P 500 has remained near last year's highs, but underneath major sector shifts are occurring, with investors dumping software and internet stocks and buying up energy and semiconductor names.

Companies that had short stints as highly anticipated IPOs, like Klarna (KLAR), are now in deeply oversold territory. Since going public last September at $40 per share, Klarna has lost two-thirds of its value. It's staggering to remember that in 2021, Klarna raised money as a private company at a >$45 billion valuation, and today sits at roughly 10% of its former heights. But while we certainly agree that hype fed into the stock's overvaluation, when we take a sober look at Klarna today, we find a business that is rapidly scaling, growing its profits, and likely oversold due to irrational fear.

I had been very cautious on Klarna immediately upon its IPO, but I upgraded the stock to a buy rating in December, when it was trading around $30 per share. Now, with shares of Klarna rapidly losing half of their value in the space of a month, we're facing heavily oversold conditions in Klarna that aren't commensurate with the company's actual fundamental performance. We can now substantiate Klarna's valuation purely from its bottom line. With this in mind, I'm reiterating my buy opinion on the stock.

Klarna is approaching bargain stock territory

First, let's discuss the elephant in the room that knocked the stock sharply down post-earnings: the perception of a weak outlook for 2026. While we do agree that Klarna's implied slowdown in growth rates is a concern, we also have to take into account not only its own scale (making deceleration very likely), but the fact that Klarna's sinking valuation already prices in substantial risk.

As shown in the chart above, Klarna is guiding to >$155 billion in GMV. Wall Street had been hoping for $158.9 billion in GMV, so that was strike one against the company. Q1 GMV guidance also has GMV decelerating to 26-30% y/y growth, versus 32% y/y growth in Q4. We should note, however, that Klarna is guiding to a minimum of $155 billion in GMV. The company is likely baking in conservatism from a shaky global macro environment (which the recent Supreme Court strikedown of U.S. tariffs may help with). It may not be long until Klarna showcases above-guidance performance (as it did in Q4) and boosts its outlook beyond the ~$159 billion that Wall Street had hoped for anyway.

The company is also guiding to a minimum revenue take rate of 2.80%. Again using the company's minimum guidance statements here, full-year revenue would clock in at $4.34 billion (against $155 billion in GMV, and representing 24% y/y growth). An adjusted operating margin of >6.9% also means a minimum of $299.5 million in adjusted profits.

That's a sizable profit threshold, especially relative to Klarna's sinking valuation. At current post-earnings share prices near $13, Klarna trades at a market cap of $4.95 billion. Netting off the $16.56 billion of cash, debt securities, and consumer receivables on Klarna's balance sheet against $15.10 billion in consumer deposits, debt and merchant payables gives us an enterprise value of $3.39 billion.

This is a multiple of just 11.3x EV/FY26 adjusted operating profits. And again, the adjusted operating profit projection is based on the company's minimum guidance thresholds. Barring any major macro shocks that cause sharp GMV deterioration or unexpected credit losses, we think Klarna has a great shot at outperforming its initial outlook: while investors have a chance to buy a onetime red-hot unicorn at a deeply discounted price.

Q4 download

Let's now go through Klarna's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Klarna's GMV grew 32% y/y to $38.70 billion. This is the core growth driver for the company: the more transactions that are processed through the Klarna network, the greater Klarna's chance to deploy its balance sheet in financing deals while reaping transaction processing fee income. As shown in the chart below, Klarna's GMV has seen a consistent pattern of acceleration, improving seven points from 25% yards/y growth in Q3. So while Q1 guidance (calling for 26-30% growth) certainly does represent deceleration from Q4, it's certainly not a bad result, and still at a stronger pace than every other quarter in FY25 barring Q4.

Where Klarna tripped up against Street estimates, however, was on transaction margin dollars (TMD). As a reminder, this is a custom metric for Klarna that is essentially the company's version of "gross margin." It subtracts transactional costs from Klarna's total revenue (from both transaction fees and interest income), but it importantly also includes an upfront charge for credit loss provisions (the estimated losses on the loan portfolio, expensed upfront upon origination).

This means that the better Klarna does on originations, the worse its TMD metric will be in the near term. That said, loans that are originated in Q4 will get the headwind of the credit provision in the same quarter, but will be generating interest income for several quarters over the life of that loan.

Klaras transaction margin dollars of $372 million in Q4 grew 17% y/y, which fell below the company's guidance of $390-$400 million. Importantly, this wasn't due to a lack of GMV or revenue take rates, both of which beat the company's estimates, but due to far stronger adoption of Fair Financing lending deals in the U.S., which is a favorable catalyst for long-term interest income growth. Overall Fair Financing transactions grew 154% y/y to $4.50 billion in GMV (12% of the company's total, doubling from 6% in the year-ago quarter).

As such, we're not at all worried about the near-term TMD miss, as it's primarily driven by the company's conservative upfront provision accounting.

Meanwhile, we note that the company is achieving improving credit quality metrics in the U.S. market. As shown below, delinquency rates for loans that are both 30+ and 60+ days past due in the 2023/2024 loan vintages are all trending downward over time:

Also important for investors to note is that Klarna is deepening its digital banking footprint in Europe. As the company's Fair Financing deals grow, it's critical as well that Klarna is able to keep a pipeline of low-cost deposits to fund its loans, allowing the company to build a healthy net interest income spread. The company's total number of banking customers doubled y/y in Q4, while total deposits grew 37% y/y to $13.0 billion:

Risks and key takeaways

In my view, much of Klarna's near-term losses are due to fear, as well as a lack of understanding about why the company is missing transaction margin dollar estimates (driven by the success of Fair Financing deals).

Of course, there are risks that we need to keep in mind here. The global macroeconomy remains shaky, and a near-term recession would hit both GMV as well as increase loan losses. That said, when Klarna is trading at ~11x forward operating income (using the conservative minimum thresholds for the company's FY26 guidance), it's hard not to acknowledge that the risk is already fully priced in.

Take advantage of oversold conditions to buy this stock.
